examples.title: JSON.stringify should disallow BigInt value
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: JSON.stringify should disallow BigInt value
Description: ### ⚙ Compilation target

es2023

### ⚙ Library

lib.es5.d.ts

### Missing / Incorrect Definition

`JSON.stringify` is correctly marked with `@throws {TypeError} If a circular reference or a BigInt value is found.`, but it types its input `value` parameter as `any`. That type could be improved so that BigInt can't even be passed into the function (turning one runtime error into a compiletime error). 

Perhaps even every type that contains a BigInt can and should be excluded. I'm not very good at TypeScript but that might be possible.

Note that if a replacer function is specified, this complicates the situation somewhat.

This issue is distinct from the perennial proposal to correctly treat `undefined` in JSON.stringify (eg https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/18879). Hopefully, the false-positive impact of this one should be much lower, and thus it can be accepted.

### Sample Code

```TypeScript
//These are runtime errors, but TypeScript doesn't complain.
//"Uncaught TypeError: Do not know how to serialize a BigInt"
JSON.stringify(1n);
JSON.stringify([1n]);
```

### Documentation Link

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Errors/BigInt_not_serializable

https://tc39.es/ecma262/multipage/structured-data.html#sec-json.stringify

https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Global_Objects/JSON/stringify

What type would the argument to `JSON.stringify` have?  If it tried to list only things that were guaranteed to be JSON stringifyable, that would be https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/1897.  If you wanted `Exclude<unknown, bigint>`, that would be https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/4196.

This issue has been marked as "Duplicate" and has seen no recent activity. It has been automatically closed for house-keeping purposes.
Symptoms: JSON.stringify should disallow BigInt value; `JSON.stringify` is correctly marked with `@throws {TypeError} If a circular reference or a BigInt value is found.`, but it types its input `value` parameter as `any`. That type could be improved so that BigInt can't even be passed into the function (turning one runtime error into a compiletime error).; //These are runtime errors, but TypeScript doesn't complain.; //"Uncaught TypeError: Do not know how to serialize a BigInt"; https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/JavaScript/Reference/Errors/BigInt_not_serializable

examples.title: kube-apiserver upgrade from v1.32 to v1.33 can deadlock due to admission webhook
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: kube-apiserver upgrade from v1.32 to v1.33 can deadlock due to admission webhooks and new, default-enabled APIs like ServiceCIDR
Description: ### What happened?

Kube-apiserver upgrade from v1.32 to v1.33 can deadlock due to admission webhooks and new, default-enabled APIs like ServiceCIDR.

When upgrading a Kubernetes cluster from v1.32 to v1.33, the kube-apiserver fails to start, causing the rolling upgrade to hang indefinitely.

The failure is caused by a post-start hook (start-kubernetes-service-cidr-controller) being denied by an admission webhook (in our case, Kyverno). The webhook rejects the request because it cannot find the new ServiceCIDR resource type when it performs a discovery check against an older, not-yet-upgraded v1.32 API server.

```
W0226 17:21:26.231132       1 dispatcher.go:226] rejected by webhook "validate.kyverno.svc-fail": &errors.StatusError{ErrStatus:v1.Status{TypeMeta:v1.TypeMeta{Kind:"", APIVersion:""}, ListMeta:v1.ListMeta{SelfLink:"", ResourceVersion:"", Continue:"", RemainingItemCount:(*int64)(nil)}, Status:"Failure", Message:"admission webhook \"validate.kyverno.svc-fail\" denied the request: resource servicecidrs not found in group networking.k8s.io/v1", Reason:"", Details:(*v1.StatusDetails)(nil), Code:400}}
I0226 17:21:26.231311       1 httplog.go:134] "HTTP" verb="POST" URI="/apis/networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs" latency="4.585229ms" userAgent="kube-apiserver/v1.33.3 (linux/amd64) kubernetes/ca991cd" audit-ID="760b32ad-b8fa-4f92-8fac-3d9e2fee3bc8" srcIP="[::1]:45386" resp=400 contentType="application/vnd.kubernetes.protobuf" apf_pl="exempt" apf_fs="exempt" apf_iseats=1 apf_fseats=0 apf_additionalLatency="0s" apf_execution_time="4.346415ms" resp=400
I0226 17:21:26.231465       1 event.go:389] "Event occurred" object="" fieldPath="" kind="ServiceCIDR" apiVersion="networking.k8s.io/v1" type="Warning" reason="KubernetesDefaultServiceCIDRError" message="The default ServiceCIDR can not be created"
I0226 17:21:26.326527       1 healthz.go:280] poststarthook/start-service-ip-repair-controllers,poststarthook/start-kubernetes-service-cidr-controller check failed: readyz
[-]poststarthook/start-service-ip-repair-controllers failed: not finished
[-]poststarthook/start-kubernetes-service-cidr-controller failed: not finished
```

### What did you expect to happen?

The kube-apiserver should start successfully during a rolling upgrade, allowing the upgrade to proceed without manual intervention. The control plane should be able to handle the initialization of new resources in a mixed-version environment gracefully.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Start with a Kubernetes v1.32 cluster.
Install an admission webhook controller (e.g., Kyverno) that has a webhook configured with failurePolicy: Fail and a broad scope that would intercept new cluster-scoped resources.
Initiate a rolling upgrade of the control plane from v1.32 to v1.33.
Observe the first upgraded kube-apiserver pod. It will fail to become ready, and its logs will show the errors mentioned above.

### Anything else we need to know?

New Feature in v1.33: The MultiCIDRServiceAllocator feature graduates to GA and is enabled by default in v1.33. 

API Version Mismatch: The networking.k8s.io/v1/servicecidrs API endpoint exists in v1.33, but not in v1.32.

Rolling Upgrade Dynamics: During a rolling upgrade, the new v1.33 kube-apiserver pod starts while the older v1.32 kube-apiserver pods are still running.

The Trigger: The v1.33 kube-apiserver's post-start hooks (start-kubernetes-service-cidr-controller) attempt to create the default ServiceCIDR resource.

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
# paste output here
```

</details>

<details>

</details>

<details>

$ cat /etc/os-release
# paste output here
$ uname -a
# paste output here

# On Windows:
C:\> wmic os get Caption, Version, BuildNumber, OSArchitecture
# paste output here
```

</details>

examples.title: apiserver: streaming version conversion for typed LIST objects (streaming JSON e
examples.old_canonical_text: Title: apiserver: streaming version conversion for typed LIST objects (streaming JSON encode still materializes whole lists) Description: ## Problem Kubernetes already supports *streaming LIST encoding* for JSON collections (ref: https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/issues/5116), but the response path still performs a **whole-list version conversion** first for typed lists (e.g. internal `core.PodList` -> external `v1.PodList`). This materializes the full external `Items []T` in memory before bytes are streamed, so peak RSS/live heap is still dominated by conversion. https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f830e4a2b8f02ca00bb51417d978ce2876e5e30e/staging/src/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/runtime/serializer/versioning/versioning.go#L271-L275 https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/f830e4a2b8f02ca00bb51417d978ce2876e5e30e/pkg/apis/core/v1/zz_generated.conversion.go#L6632-L6645 - typed list object (a Go struct list type like v1.PodList with Items []v1.Pod , as opposed to unstructured.UnstructuredList ) pprof shows a large share of allocations/retained memory in typed list conversion paths (e.g. generated `autoConvert_*List_To_*List`). ## Proposed direction When streaming JSON encoding is enabled, the server should not need to construct an entire external typed list in memory. Ideally: - convert list header once - convert items one-by-one - stream-encode items one-by-one into the JSON `items` array ## Benchmark I evaluated this under a local `local-up` apiserver setup with a synthetic large LIST workload: - **Dataset**: **30,000 Pods** in the cluster - **Load shape**: sustained LIST traffic with **staged concurrency** (1 → 5 → 10 → 20 → 25), **30s per stage** - **Measurements**: - process **RSS** over time - Go **memstats** maxima (heap_inuse / heap_sys / next_gc) - client-side LIST latency distribution (p50/p95/max) - **Profiling**: captured heap/allocs pprof snapshots around peak memory to compare conversion hotspots between baseline and experiment ## Results (30k pods, stages 1→25, 30s each) ### Summary table | Metric | Current (control) | Streamed conversion (experiment) | Delta | |---|---:|---:|---:| | RSS avg | 2.3GiB | 1.6GiB | -30.5% | | RSS max | 5.9GiB | 3.2GiB | -46.2% | | RSS min | 244.7MiB | 241.2MiB | -1.4% | | HeapInuse max | 4.6GiB | 2.4GiB | -47.4% | | HeapSys max | 5.9GiB | 3.2GiB | -45.4% | | NextGC max | 5.2GiB | 2.7GiB | -48.6% | | LIST latency p50 (avg) | 1.881s | 1.995s | +6.0% | | LIST latency p95 (avg) | 2.264s | 2.119s | -6.4% | | LIST latency max | 4.906s | 4.427s | -9.8% | ## Figures ### 1) RSS timeline (current) ### 2) RSS timeline (streamed conversion) ### 3) pprof (current vs streamed conversion) ## Why this matters For large typed LIST responses under concurrency, “streaming LIST encoding” alone is insufficient if the server still materializes the full external typed list during version conversion. Streaming conversion (header + per-item) significantly reduces peak RSS and live heap, improving stability under sustained LIST pressure. Implementation PR: #139051 /cc @xigang @serathius /sig api-machinery /kind feature /kind feature /cc @Jefftree Symptoms: apiserver: streaming version conversion for typed LIST objects (streaming JSON encode still materializes whole lists); client-side LIST latency distribution (p50/p95/max); | LIST latency p50 (avg) | 1.881s | 1.995s | +6.0% |; | LIST latency p95 (avg) | 2.264s | 2.119s | -6.4% |; | LIST latency max | 4.906s | 4.427s | -9.8% | Severity: unknown Status: open Incident type: performance Environment: {'source': 'github', 'repository': 'kubernetes/kubernetes', 'repository_owner': 'kubernetes', 'repository_name': 'kubernetes'} Affected components: kubernetes/kubernetes Tags: kind/feature, sig/api-machinery, triage/accepted

examples.title: When eventRecordQPS is set to 0, the actual QPS is 5
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: When eventRecordQPS is set to 0, the actual QPS is 5
Description: ### What happened?

When setting eventRecordQPS or eventBurst to 0 in KubeletConfiguration the documentation states that no limit is enforced. What happens instead is that eventRecordQPS has the same meaning as "event-qps" kubelet flag. The value is used directly: https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/330b5a2b8dbd681811cb8235947557c99dd8e593/cmd/kubelet/app/server.go#L597

The kubernetes client-go API interprets the value of 0 as DefaultQPS which is 5.
The same thing applies to eventBurst as well.

### What did you expect to happen?

There should be no limit when the values are set to 0.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Set eventRecordQPS/eventBurst to 0 in KubeletConfiguration.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
# paste output here
```

</details>

### Cloud provider

<details>

</details>

```console
# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
# paste output here
$ uname -a
# paste output here

# On Windows:
C:\> wmic os get Caption, Version, BuildNumber, OSArchitecture
# paste output here
```

</details>

### Install tools

<details>

</details>

### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

<details>

</details>

### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

<details>

</details>

/sig node
/assign

/sig api-machinery

It is worth mentioning that eventBurst is only used when eventRecordQPS > 0, and it should not exceed eventRecordQPS, so it cannot be set to 0

examples.title: DRA kubelet plugin helper: Allow driver names longer than 28 characters with rol
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: DRA kubelet plugin helper: Allow driver names longer than 28 characters with rolling updates enabled
Description: ### What would you like to be added?

The DRA kubelet plugin [`Helper`](https://pkg.go.dev/k8s.io/dynamic-resource-allocation/kubeletplugin#Helper) should allow drivers to enable rolling updates with driver names longer than 28 characters.

### Why is this needed?

Currently, the `Helper` includes a UID in the name of the kubelet plugin registration socket, resulting in a name of the form `{kubelet_registry_path}/{driver_name}-{uid}-reg.sock`, e.g. `/var/lib/kubelet/plugins_registry/gpu.example.com-f358c92b-e974-4f29-a84f-6c64bec0949d-reg.sock`.

With the default registry path at `/var/lib/kubelet/plugins_registry` and a 36-character UID, 28 characters are left for the driver name to stay under the [108-character limit](https://manpages.ubuntu.com/manpages/resolute/man7/unix.7.html#Address_format).

The DRA example driver [wants to use a `*.sigs.k8s.io` name](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/dra-example-driver/issues/174) for the driver and `dra-example-driver.sigs.k8s.io` is 30 characters long, causing the driver to fail to start with an error like this:

```
Error: start registrar: start gRPC server: listen on "/var/lib/kubelet/plugins_registry/gpu.dra-example-driver.sigs.k8s.io-ad4af911-5bcd-47c5-a554-f35cea869472-reg.sock": listen unix /var/lib/kubelet/plugins_registry/gpu.dra-example-driver.sigs.k8s.io-ad4af911-5bcd-47c5-a554-f35cea869472-reg.sock: bind: invalid argument
```

/sig node
/wg device-management

This issue is currently awaiting triage.

If a SIG or subproject determines this is a relevant issue, they will accept it by applying the `triage/accepted` label and provide further guidance.

The `triage/accepted` label can be added by org members by writing `/triage accepted` in a comment.

<details>

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes-sigs/prow](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/prow/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

/assign

**Proposed approach:**

Today with `RollingUpdate(...)` enabled, the helper defaults registrar socket filename to `<driverName>-<podUID>-reg.sock`.  
With longer driver names. The full path under `/var/lib/kubelet/plugins_registry` can exceed Unix socket path length limits (typically ~108) which causes `listen unix ...: bind: invalid argument`.

I propose to keep non-rolling behavior unchanged and only change the rolling-update default registrar filename to a short deterministic one:

`dra-<hex(sha256(driverName + NUL + podUID)[:16 bytes])>-reg.sock`

Why this shape:
- Bounded, short basename to stay under AF_UNIX path limits
- Deterministic per `(driverName, podUID)`
- Practically unique for node-local usage (128-bit truncated digest)
- Does not expose full driver name in the socket filename

Why this should be safe:
- kubelet plugin discovery uses socket presence/path; plugin identity comes from plugin registration RPC metadata, not from parsing the registrar filename
- `RegistrarSocketFilename(...)` remains available
- `RollingUpdate(...)` and explicit `RegistrarSocketFilename(...)` remain mutually exclusive as today

Validation:
- add unit test for the reported long-name case to assert the resulting full path stays within common Unix path bounds
- add deterministic-input and distinct-input tests for filename generation

If this direction sounds good, I can open the PR with this behaviour + tests.

> /assign

> Today with `RollingUpdate(...)` enabled, the helper defaults registrar socket filename to `<driverName>-<podUID>-reg.sock`. With longer driver names. > 
> I propose to keep non-rolling behavior unchanged and only change the rolling-update default regi

examples.title: WatchList on large collection can cause watch cache to be stale and consistent r
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: WatchList on large collection can cause watch cache to be stale and consistent read fallback to etcd
Description: ### What happened?

We observed a failure mode that WatchList on large collection (hundreds of thousands) of objects (e.g. KCM's WatchList on everything) can cause apiserver watch cache to be stale. Consistent read requests will wait for 3s and then fall back to etcd, which can cause very high memory utilization and even OOM.

FMU the problem is a RW lock contention in watch cache. [RLock of watch cache needs to be acquired](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.34/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/cacher.go#L606-L607) to set up a WatchList request. It invokes function [getAllEventsSinceLocked](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.34/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go#L875) which invokes function [getIntervalFromStoreLocked](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.34/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache.go#L934-L935) and [newCacheIntervalFromStore](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/release-1.34/staging/src/k8s.io/apiserver/pkg/storage/cacher/watch_cache_interval.go#L139) . newCacheIntervalFromStore is O(N*log N) time complexity and what function newCacheIntervalFromStore does is:

1. store.List(): iterates over the entire in-memory store to get all objects
2. Allocates a buffer for all items
3. Loops over every item creating watchCacheEvent structs
4. Sorts all events by key: sort.Sort(sortableWatchCacheEvents(...)) [O(N*log N)]

### What did you expect to happen?

WatchList and ConsistentReadFromCache should work well together in large cluster.
FMU it’s not necessary to hold the RLock when sorting events given we already have a shallow copy. A low hanging fruit seems to be moving sorting out of RLock's protection, we can reduce the time complexity from [O(N*log N)] to [O(N)].

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

We will try to provide a repro in the next couple days.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

1.34
FMU the behavior is the same for 1.34+

### Cloud provider

<details>
EKS
</details>

### OS version

<details>

```console
# On Linux:
$ cat /etc/os-release
# paste output here
$ uname -a
# paste output here

# On Windows:
C:\> wmic os get Caption, Version, BuildNumber, OSArchitecture
# paste output here
```

</details>

### Install tools

<details>

</details>

### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

<details>

examples.title: Use a cache to improve the performance of InterPodAffinity and PodTopologySpread
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: Use a cache to improve the performance of InterPodAffinity and PodTopologySpread
Description: When podAffinity, podAntiAffinity or topologySpreadConstraint exists in pod, SignPod is disabled. We hope pods with the same labels can reuse the pre-calculated results to improve the performance.

So we added a cache in the scheduler for reusing the pre-calculated results. The results will be updated in Reserve extension and event handler for the next pods scheduling. Users can also customize the cache size and gc policy to reduce the memory consumption.

In our production environment, we have enable the cache since 1.26 and the cache can significantly improve the scheduling performance of the relevant pods.

This issue is currently awaiting triage.

examples.title: flowcontrol: maxCL is unreachable
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: flowcontrol: maxCL is unreachable
Description: ### What happened?

With the current implementation of `maxCL`, it is possible that the value reported by `apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats` exceeds the total concurrency limit. Since the concurrency limit of a given priority level is bound by the total concurrency limit, this effectively means the value reported by the metric is too loose.

Moreover, in practice a priority level of type `Limited` cannot borrow more than the total number of seats that other priority levels are configured to lend. This means that the total concurrency limit is generally speaking a loose upper bound that can be restricted even further.

Here below are the values of `apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats` for an apiserver running with the default total concurrency limit of 600.

```bash
$ kubectl get --raw /metrics | grep apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats
# HELP apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats [ALPHA] Configured upper bound on number of execution seats available to each priority level
# TYPE apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats gauge
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="catch-all"} 613
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="exempt"} 600
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="global-default"} 649
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="leader-election"} 625
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="node-high"} 698
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="system"} 674
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="workload-high"} 698
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="workload-low"} 845
```

### What did you expect to happen?

With the default priority levels and default values, that is total concurrency limit of 600 and 343 borrowable seats in total, we expect the following upper limits

```console
$ kubectl get --raw /metrics | grep apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats
# HELP apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats [ALPHA] Configured upper bound on number of execution seats available to each priority level
# TYPE apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats gauge
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="catch-all"} 356        # = lower limit ( 13 ) + borrowable ( 343 )
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="exempt"} 600
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="global-default"} 367   # = lower limit ( 24 ) + borrowable ( 343 )
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="leader-election"} 368  # = lower limit ( 25 ) + borrowable ( 343 )
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="node-high"} 416        # = lower limit ( 73 ) + borrowable ( 343 )
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="system"} 393           # = lower limit ( 50 ) + borrowable ( 343 )
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="workload-high"} 392    # = lower limit ( 49 ) + borrowable ( 343 )
apiserver_flowcontrol_upper_limit_seats{priority_level="workload-low"} 367     # = lower limit ( 24 ) + borrowable ( 343 )
```

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

This behavior can be reproduced with a kind cluster using the default configuration
The total number of lendable seats sums up to 343, this is the maximum number of seats any limited priority level can borrow

```console
$ kind create cluster
$ kubectl get --raw /metrics | grep apiserver_flowcontrol_nominal_limit_seats
# HELP apiserver_flowcontrol_nominal_limit_seats [BETA] Nominal number of execution seats configured for each priority level
# TYPE apiserver_flowcontrol_nominal_limit_seats gauge
apiserver_flowcontrol_nominal_limit_seats{priority_level="catch-all"} 13        #  0% lendable =   0 lendable seats
apiserver_flowcontrol_nominal_limit_seats{priority_level="exempt"} 0
apiserver_flowcontrol_nominal_limit_seats{priority_level="global-default"} 49   # 50% lendable =  25 lendable seats
apiserver_flowcontrol_nominal_limit_seats{priority_level="lead

Title: Support arrays and slices in `NestedFieldNoCopy`
Description: Hello! 👋

I'm doing a Kube State Metrics' [Custom Resource State](https://github.com/kubernetes/kube-state-metrics/blob/main/docs/metrics/extend/customresourcestate-metrics.md) rewrite as an operator, which [employs](https://github.com/rexagod/crsm/commit/d8e3ab572e606c265abc7c7042960f6de7256ef6) `*unstructured.Unstructured`'s string-based resolving `NestedFieldNoCopy` API. However, it seems that the API currently lacks support for [slices and arrays](https://github.com/kubernetes/apimachinery/blob/v0.31.0/pkg/apis/meta/v1/unstructured/helpers_test.go#L121C21-L121C38), and I was wondering if there are any plans to do this in the future, or if the SIG would be open to a patch addressing the same?

The Kubernetes project currently lacks enough contributors to adequately respond to all issues.

This bot triages un-triaged issues according to the following rules:

- After 30d of inactivity since `lifecycle/stale` was applied, `lifecycle/rotten` is applied
- After 30d of inactivity since `lifecycle/rotten` was applied, the issue is closed

You can:

examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: .
Description: ### Checked other resources

- [x] This is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes the feature request.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar feature request and didn't find it.
- [x] I checked the LangChain documentation and API reference to see if this feature already exists.
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.

### Package (Required)

- [x] langchain
- [x] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [ ] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Feature Description

As LangChain agents become increasingly autonomous and reliant on remote tools, a single unresponsive API call can permanently freeze the orchestration layer.
ToolTimeoutMiddleware for LangGraph-backed agents. Instead of allowing execution to hang, the middleware bounds the execution (asyncio.wait_for in async contexts, and standard bounded threads in sync contexts). If a tool exceeds the threshold, the middleware intercepts it and returns a ToolMessage with status="error", allowing the LLM to recover instead of the entire system crashing gracefully.

### Use Case

If a tool exceeds the threshold, the middleware intercepts it and returns a ToolMessage with status="error", allowing the LLM to recover instead of the entire system crashing gracefully.

### Proposed Solution

_No response_

### Alternatives Considered

_No response_

### Additional Context

_No response_

Thanks for the thoughtful contribution. The idea is interesting, but after reviewing it we don't think it's the right fit for the core library at this time.

We're not currently accepting new middleware into the `langchain` monorepo — the package is already very crowded with a large dependency surface, and each addition increases the maintenance burden for the core team.

That said, we'd love to see this continue as a standalone package. You can publish it to PyPI and users can install and use it directly with `create_agent()`:

```python
pip install YOUR-PACKAGE
```

```python
from langchain.agents import create_agent
from YOUR_PACKAGE import YOURMODULE

agent = create_agent(
    model="anthropic:claude-opus-4-6",
    tools=[...],
    middleware=[
        YOURMODULE(
            key=value,
            ...
        ),
    ],
)
```

The middleware interface (`AgentMiddleware`) is public and stable — everything you need to build against is already exposed.

Our team is still working on finding the ideal way to recommend community integration packages to our users — if you have any feedback there, let me know.

examples.title: Naming controller fails to update `NamesAccepted` again once it turns false
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: Naming controller fails to update `NamesAccepted` again once it turns false
Description: ### What happened?

`naming` controller is not able to update the `NamesAccepted` condition even after the `SingularConflict` issue is fixed.

This code here - 
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/56d7898510f2a973f92fda13c2ba3a5e756d9621/staging/src/k8s.io/apiextensions-apiserver/pkg/controller/status/naming_controller.go#L249-L251

returns nil when `Spec.Names` is equal to `Status.AcceptedNames`.

Ideally, it should also check if `NamesAccepted` condition is not true and then trigger the reconciliation.

If [this](https://github.com/acumino/kubernetes/commit/5b66dd884bab7e85100383e1ba44c29aa934b525) fix is fine I can open the PR.

### What did you expect to happen?

Once the duplicate name is removed from the card Names, `naming` controller should update the `NamesAccepted` condition to `True`.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

1. Create a CRD that has `plural` and `singular` names different.

```bash
cat <<EOF | kubectl create -f -
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: bugs.crds-k8s.io
spec:
  conversion:
    strategy: None
  group: crds-k8s.io
  names:
    kind: Bug
    listKind: BugList
    plural: bugs
    singular: bug
  scope: Namespaced
  versions:
  - name: v1alpha1
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:
        type: object
        x-kubernetes-preserve-unknown-fields: true
    served: true
    storage: true
    subresources:
      status: {}
EOF
```

2. Ensure the CRD conditions are healthy and the `status.acceptedNames` is same as `spec.names`.
```bash
kubectl get crds bugs.crds-k8s.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="NamesAccepted")]}' | jq
{
  "lastTransitionTime": "2023-11-16T06:38:12Z",
  "message": "no conflicts found",
  "reason": "NoConflicts",
  "status": "True",
  "type": "NamesAccepted"
}
```
```bash
kubectl get crds bugs.crds-k8s.io -o jsonpath='{.status.acceptedNames}' | jq
{
  "kind": "Bug",
  "listKind": "BugList",
  "plural": "bugs",
  "singular": "bug"
}
```
3. Update the CRD spec by making singular name equal to plural
```bash
cat <<EOF | kubectl replace -f -
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: bugs.crds-k8s.io
spec:
  conversion:
    strategy: None
  group: crds-k8s.io
  names:
    kind: Bug
    listKind: BugList
    plural: bugs
    singular: bugs
  scope: Namespaced
  versions:
  - name: v1alpha1
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:
        type: object
        x-kubernetes-preserve-unknown-fields: true
    served: true
    storage: true
    subresources:
      status: {}
EOF
```
4. Check that the CRD condition `NamesAccepted` is now failing, but `status.acceptedNames` is not equal to `spec.names` which is expected.
```bash
kubectl get crds bugs.crds-k8s.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="NamesAccepted")]}' | jq
{
  "lastTransitionTime": "2023-11-16T06:51:29Z",
  "message": "\"bugs\" is already in use",
  "reason": "SingularConflict",
  "status": "False",
  "type": "NamesAccepted"
}
```
```bash
kubectl get crds bugs.crds-k8s.io -o jsonpath='{.status.acceptedNames}' | jq
{
  "kind": "Bug",
  "listKind": "BugList",
  "plural": "bugs",
  "singular": "bug"
}
```
5. Fix the CRD by making singular name not equal to the plural name.
```bash
cat <<EOF | kubectl replace -f -
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: bugs.crds-k8s.io
spec:
  conversion:
    strategy: None
  group: crds-k8s.io
  names:
    kind: Bug
    listKind: BugList
    plural: bugs
    singular: bug
  scope: Namespaced
  versions:
  - name: v1alpha1
    schema:
      openAPIV3Schema:
        type: object
        x-kubernetes-preserve-unknown-fields: true
    served: true
    storage: true
    subresources:
      status: {}
EOF
```
6. Check that the CRD condition `NamesAccepted` is still failing even though the issue is fixed.
```bash
kubectl get crds bugs.crds-k8s.io -o jsonpath='{.status.conditions[?(@.type=="NamesAccepted")]}' | jq
Symptoms: Naming controller fails to update `NamesAccepted` again once it turns false

examples.title: kubelet eviction manager: 'no observation found' for containerfs.inodesFree on Z
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: kubelet eviction manager: 'no observation found' for containerfs.inodesFree on ZFS (no fixed inodes)
Description: ## What happened

On Linux nodes using ZFS as the container filesystem (containerd data directory on ZFS), the kubelet eviction manager logs the following message every ~10 seconds:

```
I0531 17:53:16.101492  192859 helpers.go:949] "Eviction manager: no observation found for eviction signal" signal="containerfs.inodesFree"
```

This is because ZFS does not have fixed inodes — the `F_files` and `F_ffree` fields in `statfs` return 0 for ZFS datasets. As a result, cAdvisor's filesystem stats never report inode information for the container filesystem (containerfs), and `makeSignalObservations()` never creates an observation for `containerfs.inodesFree`.

However, `UpdateContainerFsThresholds()` in `pkg/kubelet/eviction/helpers.go` **unconditionally** creates `containerfs.inodesFree` thresholds, even when the source inode thresholds (`nodefs.inodesFree` / `imagefs.inodesFree`) may not have meaningful observations. This causes the eviction manager to log repeatedly every monitoring cycle.

## What you expected to happen

The `containerfs.inodesFree` threshold should not be added when the underlying filesystem does not provide meaningful inode statistics, or the log message should be suppressed to avoid log spam.

## How to reproduce it

1. Set up a Linux node with ZFS as the backing filesystem for the container runtime (e.g., `/var/lib/containerd` on ZFS, or `/var/lib/kubelet` on ZFS).
2. Run kubelet with default eviction settings (which includes `nodefs.inodesFree` and `imagefs.inodesFree` in `DefaultEvictionHard`).
3. Observe kubelet logs — the message appears every eviction-monitoring period (default 10s).

## Environment

- **Kubernetes version**: v1.36.1+k3s1 (also confirmed on older versions)
- **Kubelet version**: v1.36.1
- **Container runtime**: containerd 2.1.5
- **ZFS version**: 2.4.2-2
- **Kernel**: 7.0.9+deb14-amd64 (Debian sid/forky)
- **OS**: Debian GNU/Linux forky/sid

## Additional context

This is the same class of bug as issue #130142 / PR #137318, which fixed the same symptom for **Windows** (where inodes are also not supported). That fix added a guard in `UpdateContainerFsThresholds` to skip creating `containerfs.inodesFree` thresholds when the source inode thresholds (`nodefs.inodesFree` / `imagefs.inodesFree`) don't exist. The same guard is needed for ZFS on Linux.

Prior fix: PR #67709 fixed the equivalent issue for `nodefs.inodesFree` on Windows. The `containerfs.inodesFree` signal was introduced later and did not include the same guard.

The fix should mirror PR #137318: in `UpdateContainerFsThresholds`, check if the hard/soft threshold signals for `nodefs.inodesFree` or `imagefs.inodesFree` are actually set (i.e., `hardNodeINodeDisk.Signal != ""`) before adding `containerfs.inodesFree` thresholds.

/sig node
/triage accepted
/priority backlog

Would you be open to proposing a fix?

Hi , I'd like to take this. The fix looks straightforward: mirror the guard from PR #137318 in `UpdateContainerFsThresholds` to skip `containerfs.inodesFree` thresholds when the source inode signals aren't set. Will have a PR up shortly.

/assign

This is impacting me also, running ZFS root for K3S host, also using ZFS storage backed Pod hostPath volume mounts for K3S v1.36.1+k3s1, containerd 2.2.2 on Ubuntu 26.04 7.0.0-22-generic.
Symptoms: kubelet eviction manager: 'no observation found' for containerfs.inodesFree on ZFS (no fixed inodes)
Severity: unknown
Status: open
Incident type: bug
Environment: {'source': 'github', 'repository': 'kubernetes/kubernetes', 'repository_owner': 'kubernetes', 'repository_name': 'kubernetes'}
Affected components: kubernetes/kubernetes
Tags: priority/backlog, sig/node, triage/accepted

examples.title: AgentExecutor._execute_model_async uses ainvoke (non-streaming) — on_llm_new_tok
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: AgentExecutor._execute_model_async uses ainvoke (non-streaming) — on_llm_new_token never fires; LangGraph use_astream=True does not enable token-level streaming
Description: ### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a bug, not a usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes this issue.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar question and didn't find it.
- [x] I am sure that this is a bug in LangChain rather than my code.
- [x] The bug is not resolved by updating to the latest stable version of LangChain (or the specific integration package).
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.
- [x] I posted a self-contained, minimal, reproducible example. A maintainer can copy it and run it AS IS.

### Package (Required)

- [x] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [ ] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Related Issues / PRs

## Bug Description

`AgentExecutor._execute_model_async` calls `llm.ainvoke()` internally,
which is a non-streaming single-shot call. As a result, the
`on_llm_new_token` callback never fires during agent execution.

LangGraph's `use_astream=True` only controls graph-node-level streaming,
not token-by-token model output streaming.

---

## Steps to Reproduce

```python
from langchain.agents import AgentExecutor, create_react_agent
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.callbacks import BaseCallbackHandler

class TokenPrinter(BaseCallbackHandler):
    def on_llm_new_token(self, token: str, **kwargs):
        print(f"TOKEN: {token}")  # Never fires

llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o", streaming=True)
agent = create_react_agent(llm, tools, prompt)
executor = AgentExecutor(agent=agent, tools=tools, callbacks=[TokenPrinter()])

# on_llm_new_token is never called
await executor.ainvoke({"input": "What is 2 + 2?"})
```

---

## Expected Behavior

`on_llm_new_token` should fire for each token when `streaming=True`
is set on the LLM, regardless of whether the agent uses `ainvoke`
or `astream` internally.

## Actual Behavior

`on_llm_new_token` never fires. The callback receives only
`on_llm_start` and `on_llm_end`. Tokens are not streamed.

---

## Root Cause (traced in source)

`AgentExecutor._execute_model_async`
→ calls `self.agent.aplan()`
→ calls `llm.ainvoke()` (non-streaming, waits for full response)
→ `on_llm_new_token` is never triggered

For token streaming, `llm.astream()` would need to be called instead.

---

## Environment

- langchain version: x.x.x
- langchain-openai version: x.x.x
- langgraph version: x.x.x
- Python version: 3.x
- OS: (e.g. macOS 14, Ubuntu 22.04)

---

## Workaround

Using `astream_events(version="v2")` works:

```python
async for event in executor.astream_events(inputs, version="v2"):
    if event["event"] == "on_chat_model_stream":
        print(event["data"]["chunk"].content, end="")
```

But `on_llm_new_token` via callbacks should also work for
backward compatibility.

### Reproduction Steps / Example Code (Python)

```python
# pip install langchain langchain-openai

import asyncio
import os
from langchain.agents import AgentExecutor, create_react_agent
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_core.callbacks import BaseCallbackHandler
from langchain_core.tools import tool
from langchain_core.prompts import PromptTemplate

# --- Minimal tool ---
@tool
def add(a: int, b: int) -> int:
    """Adds two numbers."""
    return a + b

# --- Callback that should receive tokens ---
class TokenTracker(BaseCallbackHandler):
    def __init__(self):
        self.tokens_received = []

    def on_llm_new_token(self, token: str, **kwargs):
        self.tokens_received.append(token)
        print(f"TOKEN: {repr(token)}")  # Should print 
Symptoms: AgentExecutor._execute_model_async uses ainvoke (non-streaming) — on_llm_new_token never fires; LangGraph use_astream=True does not enable token-level streaming; ### Error Message and Stack Trace (if applicable); No exception is raised. The bug is a silent failure.
Severity: unknown
Status: open
Incident type: bug
Environment: {'source': 'github', 'repository': 'langchain-ai/langchain', 'repository_owner': 'langchain-ai', 'repository_name': 'langchain'}
Affected components: langchain-ai/langchain
Tags: bug, external, langchain

examples.title: Minimum Go version in go.mod forces importers to build with that minimum toolcha
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: Minimum Go version in go.mod forces importers to build with that minimum toolchain
Description: The `go` line in the `go.mod` describes the minimal Go version used to build a module, and also controls Go runtime behavior when that behavior changes across Go versions.  https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/129688/commits/88300c406b9199ed017e1bada29951fc18e66ae1 moved Kubernetes to Go 1.24.0, and subsequently requires any importer consuming modules from Kubernetes to also upgrade to Go 1.24.0.

This is problematic for containerd.  containerd imports several modules as part of our CRI integration, including the CRI API itself, management code for CRI implementations, and modules use to test CRI.  containerd's development branch (`main`) and our planned 2.1 release currently have a minimum of Go 1.23.  We do not plan to update to 1.24 as part of the containerd 2.1 release cycle (planned for early May), so this means any further changes to CRI or supporting libraries will not be able to be included in containerd 2.1 and would instead need to wait for 2.2 (planned for early November).

This issue is currently awaiting triage.

If a SIG or subproject determines this is a relevant issue, they will accept it by applying the `triage/accepted` label and provide further guidance.

The `triage/accepted` label can be added by org members by writing `/triage accepted` in a comment.

<details>

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes-sigs/prow](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/prow/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

/sig release architecture
/area code-organization

The reason the Kubernetes go.mod `go` directives are bumped to the latest Go minor in new Kubernetes versions as soon as we adopt building with a new Go version is to ensure Kubernetes adopts / tests / supports new Go runtime behaviors as soon as possible.

https://github.com/kubernetes/enhancements/blob/master/keps/sig-release/3744-stay-on-supported-go-versions/ and https://go.dev/doc/godebug describe how the Go runtime bases its behavior on the `go.mod` file's `go` directive.

We haven't thought through keeping *some* of the staging modules' go.mod files at older `go` versions... I'm not sure offhand what all the implications of that would be... all our testing and management scripts currently assume homogenous versions among those modules, which is definitely simplifying.

cc @dims

examples.title: Proposal: Move k8s.io/cri-api out of staging for independent build and managemen
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: Proposal: Move k8s.io/cri-api out of staging for independent build and management
Description: **Problem:**

The `k8s.io/cri-api` module currently [uses go 1.24](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/v1.33.0/staging/src/k8s.io/cri-api/go.mod#L5) (as of k8s v1.33.0), while importers like containerd [continue to use 1.23](https://github.com/containerd/containerd/blob/v2.0.5/go.mod#L3). This creates a versioning conflict and forces importers to update go versions. This is further detailed in https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/130799.

**Proposal:**

We propose to move `k8s.io/cri-api` out of staging so it can be built and managed independently.

@dims identified the following items which need to happen to fix:

- remove publish-bot entries to populate the repository
- push a new tag to that repository
- remove code from staging and switch to published tag
- populate the OWNERS file etc as needed to establish control
- setup any CI or bots needed and define a release process/cadence etc

Could sig-node leads please chime in on the following and let us know if anything is missing?

/sig node
/cc @dchen1107
/cc @derekwaynecarr
/cc @mrunalp
/cc @BenTheElder

This issue is currently awaiting triage.

If a SIG or subproject determines this is a relevant issue, they will accept it by applying the `triage/accepted` label and provide further guidance.

The `triage/accepted` label can be added by org members by writing `/triage accepted` in a comment.

Instructions for interacting with me using PR comments are available [here](https://git.k8s.io/community/contributors/guide/pull-requests.md).  If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes-sigs/prow](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/prow/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

cc @haircommander @saschagrunert

We talked about this in Kubecon London as well during Maintainer Summit. Don't know if anyone captured notes. Specific questions i asked was "what is the governance around CRI API? How do we take decisions? etc.."

examples.title: golangci-lint: re-enable `newexpr` hint in modernize linter
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: golangci-lint: re-enable `newexpr` hint in modernize linter
Description: ### What would you like to be added?

Go 1.26 allows `new(x)` in place of `ptr.To(x)`. The `modernize` linter surfaces this as a hint (`newexpr`). We should hint at changes like this in master only when at least one older stable branch is also on Go 1.26.

In practice this issue can be resolved by reverting https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/137596.

### Why is this needed?

Avoid pushing developers towards features which will cause problems when the new code needs to be backported.

/sig testing
/milestone v1.37
/priority backlog
/triage accepted

examples.title: kube-proxy nftables: sync time grows super-linearly with Service count
examples.old_canonical_text: Title: kube-proxy nftables: sync time grows super-linearly with Service count Description: ### What happened? Our production clusters run kube-proxy in `ipvs` mode. We evaluated migrating to `nftables` mode and observed that `kube-proxy` takes a long time to complete a sync, with the `nft` process at 100% CPU for most of that time. This looks related to [#135639](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/135639), which was addressed in v1.36. We are testing on v1.36.1 and still see multi-minute syncs. The two dominant costs we measure are: 1. the `ListAll` (`nft --json --terse list table ip kube-proxy`) that runs on every sync, including incremental syncs, and 1. the full sync re-emitting the entire table even when almost nothing changed. #### How we measured We created different numbers of Services/EndpointSlices in a test cluster and read kube-proxy logs at `--v=2`. A sync logs three lines: ``` I0529 17:51:15.687847 1 proxier.go:1094] "Syncing nftables rules" ipFamily="IPv4" fullSync=true I0529 17:52:18.984398 1 proxier.go:1712] "Reloading service nftables data" ipFamily="IPv4" numServices=44125 numEndpoints=44152 I0529 18:07:59.283867 1 proxier.go:1088] "SyncProxyRules complete" ipFamily="IPv4" elapsed="16m43.895505566s" ``` - ListAll time is roughly the gap between `"Syncing nftables rules"` and `"Reloading service nftables data"`. During this window `nft --json --terse list table ip kube-proxy` runs at 100% CPU. (This window also includes a small stale-chain delete transaction, which is negligible at these scales.) - Total time is the `elapsed=` value on `"SyncProxyRules complete"`. #### Test environment - Instance: `m6a.4xlarge` - kube-proxy: v1.36.1, `mode: nftables`. Default sync params: `minSyncPeriod=1s syncPeriod=30s maxSyncPeriod=1h0m0s`. - calico-node: v3.31.3 - Kernel: Linux 7.0 (Ubuntu 26.04); also reproduced on Linux 7.1-rc4 - Control plane: v1.33.7 - kubelet: v1.33.7 > We ran kube-proxy v1.36.1, despite the version skew against the v1.33.7 control plane and kubelet, to include the v1.36 nftables improvements (#135800, #136499). We believe the behavior we measured does not depend on the control plane or kubelet version: it is node-local nftables programming, driven only by the number of Services/EndpointSlices. If that assumption is wrong, please let us know and we can re-test on a version-matched cluster. Our production clusters reach ~44k Services at an almost 1:1 Service-to-endpoint ratio. That is a much larger Service count than #135639's repro (~1k–5k Services, with high endpoints-per-Service). So the cost here is driven by the number of Services/chains, not just endpoint fan-out, and it appears at Service counts well beyond what #135639 tested. No production workloads run on the test cluster. To trigger incremental syncs, a script creates and deletes 50 Services every 30 seconds. #### Results `fullSync=true` (full sync) vs `fullSync=false` (incremental). Each cell is the p50 across the collected syncs at that scale; the ListAll and Total values are taken from the same sync (the one at p50 of Total). | N (services ~ endpoints) | incremental — ListAll | incremental — Total | full — ListAll | full — Total | | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | | 4,125 | 874 ms | 878 ms | 939 ms | 5.11 s | | 14,125 | 7.25 s | 7.49 s | 7.45 s | 58.25 s | | 24,125 | 20.44 s | 20.76 s | 21.48 s | 3m11.10s | | 34,125 | 39.61 s | 40.30 s | 47.19 s | 8m13.51s | | 44,125 | 1m9.01s | 1m10.14s | 1m3.30s | 16m43.90s | | 44,125 (empty kube-proxy table) | — | — | 284 ms | 4m46.84s | Observations: - From 4k to 44k Services (~11× the count), incremental sync grew ~80× and full sync grew ~197×. The growth is much steeper than linear in the number of Services. - Incremental sync is almost entirely `ListAll`. The actual write is small (<2s even at 44k); the rest is reading the whole table to discover existing chains/affinity sets for cleanup. - Full sync = `ListAll` + re-emitting Symptoms: kube-proxy nftables: sync time grows super-linearly with Service count; At 44k Services, an incremental sync takes >1 minute, so any single change (Service create, deployment rollout) has a Network Programming Latency (NPL) over a minute on that node. This is too slow for our production use case. We would like incremental NPL to be on the order of seconds (e.g. p50 ~30s or better) at this scale.; I could not execute the Linux-tagged nftables tests locally on this Darwin/arm64 host. `GOOS=linux go test ...` produced an `exec format error`, and no Docker/podman/nerdctl/colima/lima/qemu runner was installed. For that reason the PR is draft and uses `Refs #139513` rather than claiming the issue is fixed. Severity: unknown Status: open Incident type: performance Environment: {'source': 'github', 'repository': 'kubernetes/kubernetes', 'repository_owner': 'kubernetes', 'repository_name': 'kubernetes'} Affected components: kubernetes/kubernetes Tags: kind/bug, needs-triage, sig/network

examples.title: Unbounded CEL estimate on a string field bounded using allOf
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: Unbounded CEL estimate on a string field bounded using allOf
Description: ### What happened?

I added an x-kubernetes-validation rule to a bounded text field and was surprised to find it was rejected for its estimated cost.

I use controller-gen to produce the CRD, and the following schema seemed to confuse the estimator:

```
allOf:
- maxLength: 10
- maxLength: 20
```

### What did you expect to happen?

I expected a `maxLength` property to limit the cost of CEL evaluation of string fields.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Kubernetes rejects the following CRD due to the cost of its one rule:

```yaml
apiVersion: apiextensions.k8s.io/v1
kind: CustomResourceDefinition
metadata:
  name: lengths.example.com
spec:
  group: example.com
  names:
    kind: Length
    listKind: LengthList
    plural: lengths
    singular: length
  scope: Namespaced
  versions:
    - name: v1
      served: true
      storage: true
      schema:
        openAPIV3Schema:
          type: object
          properties:
            apiVersion:
              type: string
            kind:
              type: string
            metadata:
              type: object
            spec:
              type: object
              properties:
                text:
                  type: string
                  allOf:
                    - maxLength: 10
                    - maxLength: 20
                  x-kubernetes-validations:
                    - rule: true && self.contains(self)
```

```
The CustomResourceDefinition "lengths.example.com" is invalid: 
* spec.validation.openAPIV3Schema.properties[spec].properties[text].x-kubernetes-validations[0].rule: Forbidden: estimated rule cost exceeds budget by factor of more than 100x (try simplifying the rule, or adding maxItems, maxProperties, and maxLength where arrays, maps, and strings are declared)
* spec.validation.openAPIV3Schema.properties[spec].properties[text].x-kubernetes-validations[0].rule: Forbidden: contributed to estimated rule cost total exceeding cost limit for entire OpenAPIv3 schema
* spec.validation.openAPIV3Schema: Forbidden: x-kubernetes-validations estimated rule cost total for entire OpenAPIv3 schema exceeds budget by factor of more than 100x (try simplifying the rule, or adding maxItems, maxProperties, and maxLength where arrays, maps, and strings are declared)
```

### Anything else we need to know?

Adding `maxLength: 500` _outside_ the `allOf` property reduces the cost, making the CRD valid:

```diff
                  type: string
                  allOf:
                    - maxLength: 10
                    - maxLength: 20
+                 maxLength: 500
                  x-kubernetes-validations:
                    - rule: true && self.contains(self)
```

```
customresourcedefinition.apiextensions.k8s.io/lengths.example.com created
```

The controller-gen folks recommended reporting this as a bug:
- https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/controller-tools/pull/1270#issuecomment-3273391765

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.33.1
Kustomize Version: v5.6.0
Server Version: v1.33.1
```

</details>

### Cloud provider

<details>
n/a
</details>

### OS version

<details>
n/a
</details>

### Install tools

<details>
minikube
</details>

### Container runtime (CRI) and version (if applicable)

<details>
n/a
</details>

### Related plugins (CNI, CSI, ...) and versions (if applicable)

<details>
n/a
</details>

/sig api-machinery

/triage accepted
cc @jpbetz @cici37 can you confirm? this sounds like a valid bug to me.

https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/134212 proposes a fix

Thanks for raising the issue. Yes we initial only considered the maxLength/maxItems/maxProperties directly attached to the field. Thanks for the fix

examples.title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy causes Namespaces deletion to hang due to race conditi
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy causes Namespaces deletion to hang due to race condition
Description: ### What happened?

I'm implementing policies using ValidatingAdmissionPolicy and ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBindings using a custom CR as paramKind.

The binding is configured with `.spec.paramRef.parameterNotFoundAction: Deny`

Some of those policies are associated to external resources that may take a bit longer to terminate.

When a namespace is deleted, the custom CR resource is immediately removed, and the policy's target resource is left hanging.

Attempting to remove the resource's finalizer returns a ValidatingAdmissionPolicy error:
```
The persistentvolumeclaims "xxx-pvc" is invalid: : ValidatingAdmissionPolicy 'xxx-vap' with binding 'xxx-vapbind' denied request: failed to configure binding: no params found for policy binding with `Deny` parameterNotFoundAction
```

### What did you expect to happen?

The resources in the namespace to be deleted along with the namespace

Perhaps the policy should stop acting on the namespace if it is in Terminating state.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

Create Kind cluster with extraMount
```
apiVersion: kind.x-k8s.io/v1alpha4
kind: Cluster
nodes:
  - role: control-plane
    extraMounts:
      - hostPath: <some-path>/foo
        containerPath: /foo
```

Create test namespace
```
> kubectl create ns test
```

Deploy ValidatingAdmissionPolicy, ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding and ConfigMap parameter reference
```
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingAdmissionPolicy
metadata:
  name: policy
spec:
  paramKind:
    apiVersion: v1
    kind: ConfigMap
  matchConstraints:
    resourceRules:
      - apiVersions:
          - v1
        apiGroups:
          - ""
        resources:
          - persistentvolumeclaims
        operations:
          - CREATE
          - UPDATE
  validations:
    - expression: params != null && has(params.data) && has(params.data.storageClasses) && variables.storageClassName == params.data.storageClasses
      messageExpression: '"Namespace " + string(namespaceObject.metadata.name) + " does not have permissions to storageClassName " + variables.storageClassName'
  failurePolicy: Fail
  variables:
    - name: storageClassName
      expression: '"storageClassName" in object.spec ? object.spec.storageClassName : "empty"'
---
apiVersion: admissionregistration.k8s.io/v1
kind: ValidatingAdmissionPolicyBinding
metadata:
  name: binding
spec:
  policyName: policy
  paramRef:
    name: ref
    parameterNotFoundAction: Deny
  validationActions:
    - Deny
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: ConfigMap
metadata:
  name: ref
data:
  storageClasses: "standard"
```

Create the PVC
```
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolume
metadata:
  name: pv-www
spec:
  storageClassName: standard
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  capacity:
    storage: 500Mi
  hostPath:
    path: /foo/
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: pvc-www
spec:
  volumeName: pv-www
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 100Mi
---
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: nginx-deployment
  labels:
    app: nginx
spec:
  replicas: 3
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: nginx
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: nginx
    spec:
      volumes:
      - name: www
        persistentVolumeClaim:
          claimName: pvc-www
      containers:
      - name: nginx
        image: nginx:1.14.2
        volumeMounts:
        - name: www
          mountPath: /var/www
```

Delete the namespace:
```
> kubectl delete ns test
```

The namespace will hang due to the PVC, attempting to remove the PVC finalizer will reveal the ValidatingAdmissionPolicy error:
```
> kubectl patch pvc pvc-www -p '{"metadata":{"finalizers":[]}}' --type=merge
The persistentvolumeclaims "pvc-www" is invalid: : ValidatingAdmissionPolicy 'policy' with binding 'binding' denied request: failed to configure binding: no params found for policy binding with `Deny` parameterNotFoundAction
```

The PVC is removed (or final

examples.title: WAS: Run Multiple Placement Generation Plugins for the Same PodGroup
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: WAS: Run Multiple Placement Generation Plugins for the Same PodGroup
Description: Today, TAS placement generation assumes a single placement-producing path for a PodGroup. This is insufficient when multiple independent constraints must be satisfied simultaneously. To support more advanced scheduling policies, the framework should allow multiple registered placement generation plugins to run independently for the same PodGroup.

- [ ] Extend the Placement struct to include Name, Node list, and PluginSpecificData. 
- [ ] Implement the plugin execution logic to run registered placement plugins independently (in parallel).
- [ ] Implement the core framework logic to merge the []Placement results from multiple plugins (focusing on node list intersection and the combination of PluginSpecificData).
- [ ] Add integration tests for running multiple plugins

More details and discussion: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1n9IioTorBTVC1HKM2X0fQnnMRbLN0vxUGgvjnZ4S9SM/edit?resourcekey=0-nkq2Hg1DPbEcTOmqbGxurg&tab=t.0#heading=h.pinlmfgvi1yu.

/sig scheduling
/area workload-aware

/triage accepted
/cc @kubernetes/sig-scheduling-leads
/cc @mm4tt

Title: WAS: TAS Performance Improvements
Description: Improve TAS (Topology-aware Scheduling) scheduling performance and scalability by limiting the number of candidate placements considered during scheduling, then validate the impact through scalability testing and memory-consumption comparisons.

- [ ] Add support for a Max Candidate Placements option to bound the number of candidate placements evaluated by TAS.
     - Define and implement the Max Candidate Placements configuration option.
     - Add unit tests covering the new behavior.
     - Add the necessary integration tests to validate TAS behavior with the option enabled.
- [ ] Run scalability tests with the improvements to measure the performance impact of the improvements.
     1. Update existing scalability tests, or add new ones if needed, to cover TAS with the new candidate placement limit.
     2. Define SLOs based on the measurements in scalability tests, including the SLOs in the KEP (PRR section)

examples.title: containerStatuses ready stays true while started is false after container restar
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: containerStatuses ready stays true while started is false after container restart at the first time
Description: ### What happened?

In pods' ContainerStatuses, there are two state fields, ready: controlled by readinessProbe, started: controlled by startupProbe.

As designed, readinessProbe won't start working until startupProbe is succeed.

Normally the field 'ready' won't be true if 'started' is still false.

But if the container in the pod exited, and the restartPolicy is Always or OnFailure, kubelet will try restart the container process immediately without changing the state of the pod. 

That cause ContainerStatuses 'ready' stays true while 'started' is false after container restart at the first time.

### What did you expect to happen?

Set the ready state to false when the container restart at the first time.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

I reproduced this issue with a busybox configured as below, the startupProbe will pass after 30s, livenessProbe will kill the container after 60s, you can get the pod yaml after it restarted at the first time to see the fields: 

```
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  labels:
    app: busybox
  name: busybox
spec:
  selector:
    matchLabels:
      app: busybox
  replicas: 1
  template:
    metadata:
      labels:
        app: busybox
    spec:
      containers:
      - name: busybox
        image: busybox:latest
        command:
          - bash
          - -c
          - sleep 30 && echo 1 > /started && sleep 3600
        imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent
        startupProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - cat
            - /started
          failureThreshold: 30
          periodSeconds: 10
        readinessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - ls
        livenessProbe:
          exec:
            command:
            - cat
            - /live
          failureThreshold: 6
          periodSeconds: 10
      restartPolicy: Always
```

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

### Kubernetes version

<details>

```console
$ kubectl version
Client Version: v1.31.1
Kustomize Version: v5.4.2
Server Version: v1.31.1
```

</details>

### Cloud provider

```console

$ cat /etc/os-release

$ uname -a

```

</details>

### Install tools

</details>

</details>

<details>

</details>

/sig node

Hey maintainers!
I found this issue very interesting, over the past couple of days, i have been going through the codebase of Kubernetes, and have understood what the issue is and proposed a solution to this in the PR #136065 !
I would highly appreciate your suggestions / feedback on this, and please do let me know if any changes / modifications are required!
Thanks!

If you can add tests to your PR to demonstrate the repro then that can help us triage this issue. (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/136065#pullrequestreview-3634544397)

examples.title: Add deterministic middleware orchestration with priority, conditional activation
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: Add deterministic middleware orchestration with priority, conditional activation, and safe parallel execution
Description: ### Submission checklist

- [x] This is a feature request, not a bug report or usage question.
- [x] I added a clear and descriptive title that summarizes the feature request.
- [x] I used the GitHub search to find a similar feature request and didn't find it.
- [x] I checked the LangChain documentation and API reference to see if this feature already exists.
- [x] This is not related to the langchain-community package.

### Package (Required)

- [x] langchain
- [ ] langchain-openai
- [ ] langchain-anthropic
- [ ] langchain-classic
- [x] langchain-core
- [ ] langchain-model-profiles
- [ ] langchain-tests
- [ ] langchain-text-splitters
- [ ] langchain-chroma
- [ ] langchain-deepseek
- [ ] langchain-exa
- [ ] langchain-fireworks
- [ ] langchain-groq
- [ ] langchain-huggingface
- [ ] langchain-mistralai
- [ ] langchain-nomic
- [ ] langchain-ollama
- [ ] langchain-openrouter
- [ ] langchain-perplexity
- [ ] langchain-qdrant
- [ ] langchain-xai
- [ ] Other / not sure / general

### Feature Description

LangChain middleware is already a strong abstraction for cross-cutting agent concerns like guardrails, prompt transforms, tool handling, logging, and observability.

The recent dependency-management work around middleware is a strong step forward, especially because dependency declaration and resolution make larger middleware stacks easier to manage.

That said, there still seems to be a gap for production-scale middleware orchestration in three areas:

- Explicit priority-based ordering
- Conditional activation based on runtime context
- Safe parallel execution for read-only middleware, with deterministic merge after execution

Today, middleware can be stacked, but there is no first-class orchestration contract for:

1. Which middleware must run before others
2. Which middleware should activate only under specific runtime conditions
3. Which middleware is safe to run concurrently
4. How concurrent results should be merged deterministically for correctness and debugging

### Use Case

This would help when building production agents that combine multiple middleware concerns on the same agent, for example:

- safety / policy middleware
- PII redaction
- prompt/context transforms
- logging / tracing
- metrics / analytics
- provider- or tool-specific request shaping

In these setups, some middleware must run before others, some should only activate under certain runtime conditions, and some are effectively read-only and could run concurrently.

Right now, larger middleware stacks become harder to reason about because ordering relies mostly on stack composition and dependency declarations, while concurrency and deterministic merge semantics are not first-class.

This is especially relevant because AgentMiddleware hooks can run around important points in the agent loop, including before/after agent execution, before/after model execution, and wrapping model/tool calls.

### Proposed Solution

Introduce a lightweight orchestration wrapper around middleware stacks.

Example shape:

```python
from langchain.agents import create_agent
from langchain.middleware import MiddlewareSpec, MiddlewareStack

stack = MiddlewareStack(
    [
        MiddlewareSpec(
            middleware=SafetyMiddleware(),
            name="safety",
            priority=100,
            mode="sequential",
        ),
        MiddlewareSpec(
            middleware=PIIRedactionMiddleware(),
            name="pii",
            priority=90,
            depends_on=["safety"],
            mode="sequential",
        ),
        MiddlewareSpec(
            middleware=LoggingMiddleware(),
            name="logging",
            priority=10,
            mode="parallel_readonly",
        ),
        MiddlewareSpec(
            middleware=MetricsMiddleware(),
            name="metrics",
            priority=10,
            mode="parallel_readonly",
        ),
    ],
    deterministic_merge=True,
)

agent = create_agent(
    model=model,
    tools=tools,
    middleware=stack,
)
```

Sug

Incident type: bug

examples.title: Better field-ordering when rendering YAML/JSON
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: Better field-ordering when rendering YAML/JSON
Description: Today it looks like fields are all rendered in alphabetical order which makes for terrible reading.  We have a strong convention of list-map fields -- when we render each item, it would be much nicer to put the key(s) first.

Example, compare:

```
    conditions:
    - lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:34Z"
      status: "False"
      type: PodReadyToStartContainers
    - lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:32Z"
      status: "True"
      type: Initialized
    - lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:32Z"
      reason: PodFailed
      status: "False"
      type: Ready
    - lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:32Z"
      reason: PodFailed
      status: "False"
      type: ContainersReady
    - lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:32Z"
      status: "True"
      type: PodScheduled
```

with

```
    conditions:
    - type: PodReadyToStartContainers
      lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:34Z"
      status: "False"
    - type: Initialized
      lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:32Z"
      status: "True"
    - type: Ready
      lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:32Z"
      reason: PodFailed
      status: "False"
    - type: ContainersReady
      lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:32Z"
      reason: PodFailed
      status: "False"
    - type: PodScheduled
      lastProbeTime: null
      lastTransitionTime: "2024-06-09T22:52:32Z"
      status: "True"
```

It would super if we could annotate fields with enough information to get
better ordering.  Perhaps a simple integer, such that lower numbers render
first, and any two fields with the same number arte sorted alphabetically.

examples.title: ChatOpenAI drops `reasoning_content` from OSS models (like vLLM/DeepSeek) during
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: ChatOpenAI drops `reasoning_content` from OSS models (like vLLM/DeepSeek) during AIMessage conversion
Description: ### Description

When using `langchain_openai.ChatOpenAI` to connect to an OpenAI-compatible endpoint that serves open-source reasoning models (e.g., via `vLLM` or `Ollama`), the `reasoning_content` field returned in the raw JSON response is silently dropped.

Many users serve models like DeepSeek-R1 via vLLM, which utilizes the `reasoning_content` field for the model's thought process. However, because `_convert_dict_to_message` in `langchain_openai/chat_models/base.py` hardcodes the extraction of `content`, `function_call`, `tool_calls`, and `audio`, any additional non-standard fields like `reasoning_content` are destroyed before the `AIMessage` is returned.

### Steps to Reproduce

1. Initialize `ChatOpenAI` pointing to a local `vLLM` server serving a reasoning model (e.g., `DeepSeek-R1`).
```python
from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI

llm = ChatOpenAI(
    model="deepseek-r1-20b",
    openai_api_base="http://localhost:8000/v1",
    openai_api_key="EMPTY"
)

response = llm.invoke("What is 10 + 10? Please think step by step.")
```
2. The raw response from vLLM contains `reasoning_content` in the `choices[0].message` payload.
3. Check `response.additional_kwargs`. The `reasoning_content` is missing, and `response.content` only contains the final output without the reasoning trace.

### Expected Behavior

`ChatOpenAI` should either:
1. Dump unhandled payload fields into `additional_kwargs` (e.g., `additional_kwargs["reasoning_content"] = _dict.get("reasoning_content")`) so that users can still access custom fields.
2. Provide a parameter like `include_extra=True` to preserve extra fields from the raw provider response.

### Code snippet where the issue occurs:
In `_convert_dict_to_message` (`langchain_openai/chat_models/base.py`):
```python
    if role == "assistant":
        content = _dict.get("content", "") or ""
        additional_kwargs: dict = {}
        
        if function_call := _dict.get("function_call"):
            additional_kwargs["function_call"] = dict(function_call)
            
        tool_calls = []
        invalid_tool_calls = []
        if raw_tool_calls := _dict.get("tool_calls"):
            # ... parses tool calls ...

        if audio := _dict.get("audio"):
            additional_kwargs["audio"] = audio
            
        # reasoning_content is ignored!
            
        return AIMessage(
            content=content,
            additional_kwargs=additional_kwargs,
            name=name,
            id=id_,
            tool_calls=tool_calls,
            invalid_tool_calls=invalid_tool_calls,
        )
```

### Workaround
Currently, users are forced to use `ChatDeepSeek` (which overrides this method to preserve `reasoning_content`), even if the model isn't strictly a DeepSeek model, just to preserve the vLLM response format. Passing `additional_kwargs` dynamically in the base class would resolve this limitation natively.

### Environment
* `langchain-openai==1.2.2`
* `langchain-core==1.4.1`
* `langchain==1.3.4`

This issue was automatically closed because it appears to have been submitted programmatically — issue types are automatically set when using the GitHub web interface, and this issue has none.

We do not allow automated issue submission at this time.

Please use one of the [issue templates](https://github.com/langchain-ai/langchain/issues/new/choose).

examples.title: Pod template hash can be shorter than expected, depending on the spec
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: Pod template hash can be shorter than expected, depending on the spec
Description: ### What happened?

`controller.ComputeHash` method ([ref](https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/6eee830d4fa0cdb7d75dac4b3b4d22d639dce54b/pkg/controller/controller_utils.go#L1395)) used to get the hash of PodTemplateSpec (used by daemonset and deployment controllers) can return shorter hash than expected 10 chars, depending on the `PodTemplateSpec` that is passed to this func. Some users might rely on specific length of the hash that is appended to the `ReplicaSet` or `ControllerRevision` names.

/sig apps

### What did you expect to happen?

The hash to be of the exact same length regardless of PodTemplateSpec used.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

I found this during Workload API implementation (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/134564). Adding a new field to the pod spec changed the hash in the tests. Manipulating with the labels can lead to correct or incorrect hash length (see https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/134564#discussion_r2439332948).

### Anything else we need to know?

ComputeHash is encoding the hash number (`podTemplateSpecHasher.Sum32()`) into the string using `rand.SafeEncodeString`. When this issue occurs, the hash number has 9 digits, not 10, what leads to the shorter hash.
https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/6eee830d4fa0cdb7d75dac4b3b4d22d639dce54b/pkg/controller/controller_utils.go#L1395-L1406

If you have questions or suggestions related to my behavior, please file an issue against the [kubernetes-sigs/prow](https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/prow/issues/new?title=Prow%20issue:) repository.
</details>

We identified the same issue yesterday. After discussing with Dynatrace support, we were informed that the agents only look for ReplicaSets with hashes longer than 8 characters. In our environment, most ReplicaSets have 10-character hashes. However, we have observed that sometimes, after a simple rollout restart of a deployment, even without changing any configuration, some hashes are only 7 characters long. As a result, this leads to a monitoring outage for the affected application.

Kubernetes version : 1.30.8
OS Version : Ubuntu 20.04
CNI : Calico 3.28.2
CloudProvider : OnPremise (Openstack)

The problem seems to be related with : https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/121687

This is related to https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/116447 and https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/issues/115489 -- like it or not there are several downstream projects which (wrongly) try to parse pod names and that often involves a regex.

On one hand, here I can point to new evidence that it was always wrong, and they just had not stumbled on it yet.  On the other hand it's somewhat ecosystem-hostile.

We should, at the very least, fix our own tests which assume exactly 10 characters.

examples.title: `maskTrailingDash` doesn't match its intended behavior
examples.old_canonical_text:
Title: `maskTrailingDash` doesn't match its intended behavior
Description: ### What happened?

`maskTrailingDash` (https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/blob/master/staging/src/k8s.io/apimachinery/pkg/api/validation/generic.go#L80-L85) is supposed to replace the final character of the (generate)name with "a" if the final character is "-". For example, "foo-" should be changed to "fooa". However, the code replaces the last two characters with "a", so "foo-" is changed to "foa".

This seems to be a bug that lets an invalid (generate)name pass. For example, "a?-" will be accepted, but the generated name "a?-vwxyz" is invalid.

### What did you expect to happen?

This can be simply fixed by replacing the final character only. I can send a PR for this.

### How can we reproduce it (as minimally and precisely as possible)?

The bug is clear as the code shows.

### Anything else we need to know?

_No response_

master

looking at the historical context https://github.com/kubernetes/kubernetes/pull/101626#discussion_r624043549 this seems intentional. cc @liggitt.
